posts.caption:
For its new Autumn 2026 campaign, Escape to the Countryside, @burberry's idea of Britishness becomes a road trip through the rolling British hills, ending at Deene Park in Northamptonshire. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Among the friends folded into Burberry‚Äôs dream of town-to-country freedom is @mosesmartin, making his first ever fashion campaign appearance alongside Edie Campbell, Nora Attal and Sang Woo Kim. Martin, the son of Gwyneth Paltrow and Chris Martin, comes with a famous surname, but with music in his blood he‚Äôs not standing in its shadow. His band, @peopleivemet, soundtracks the adventure. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
As he stars in the latest Burberry campaign, Martin answers questions about his band, the outdoors and his favourite British food. Read more at the link in bio üì≤‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üì∏ #Burberry Autumn 2026 Campaign. Courtesy of Burberry‚ÅÝ
posts.alt_text:
From Wexford in Ireland, Central Saint Martins graduate @richardmalone imbues their colourful pieces with traditional craftsmanship, using skills passed down through their maternal lineage. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
In 2015, Malone debuted at London Fashion Week with Fashion East, and more recently, their fashion practice has stretched into sculpture ‚Äì Malone‚Äôs billowing indigo piece took centre stage at The Royal Academy‚Äôs 2023 Summer Exhibition. Now, they are heading up an ambitious project for Ireland‚Äôs Presidency of the Council of the European Union 2026. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Cuimhne agus S√©adchomhartha (Memory and Monument) will show across several official spaces in Brussels, spotlighting a host of Irish artists while uncovering shared material histories throughout Europe. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm really interested in this recent Irish renaissance, which is also quite farcical,‚Äù they tell @emilycsteer, when we speak ahead of the opening. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs really focused on the industries that produce lots of cash: major movie stars, people winning Oscars ‚Ä¶ ‚Äù‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Read the interview at the link in bio üì≤‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üì∏ Lead image: Richard Malone. Photography by @donal.talbot‚ÅÝ
2-7. Cuimhne agus S√©adchomhartha installation imagery ¬© @hugardandvanoverschelde‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
#RichardMalone‚ÅÝ

posts.caption:
@sofiacoppola has long understood the rich texture of adolescence. @importantflowers, the publishing imprint she established with @mack_publishing, extends that eye across film, photography, art and fashion, and its latest title feels at home. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Here, AnOther can exclusively preview American Teen, a 240-page book bringing together @josephszabophotos‚Äôs renowned and previously unseen photographs of his students on Long Island during the 1970s and 1980s, with new texts by commissioning editor Coppola and Sonic Youth‚Äôs @kimletgordon.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
At the link in bio, find out more about Joseph Szabo's work üì≤‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üì∏ Joseph Szabo, from American Teen (Important Flowers, 2026). Courtesy of the artist and @mack_publishing

posts.caption:
When she was 19, @charlottegainsbourg ‚Äúwasn‚Äôt well. I was a mess‚Äù. Her famous father, Serge Gainsbourg ‚Äì France‚Äôs beloved chanteur-provocateur ‚Äì invited her to move back in with him and recuperate at his home, 5 bis rue de Verneuil in Paris. ‚ÄúEverything was ready for me to step back into my childhood house with him and try to pull the pieces of my life together,‚Äù she recalls. ‚ÄúThen he died so suddenly.‚Äù‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
For some 30 years, Charlotte kept her father‚Äôs house preserved as he‚Äôd left it ‚Äì a ‚Äúprivate mausoleum‚Äù where she could visit and grieve, away from the cemetery and the curious gaze and unwanted attentions of the general public. Then, in 2023, the house became a museum. But before Maison Gainsbourg opened its doors as a heritage site, Charlotte used her Hasselblad camera to document the place. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Now, these evocative pictures are on display in her debut photography exhibition, 5 Bis, curated with the help of @ysl creative director @anthonyvaccarello. The show, running as part of this year‚Äôs world-renowned @rencontresarles festival, is held in The Cloister Gallery ‚Äì a sequestered space entered via a doorway on one of the medieval town‚Äôs narrow, picturesque streets. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
At the link in bio, @ohnoitsemilyd speaks with Gainsbourg about immortalising her father‚Äôs Parisian home as she remembered it from her youth üì≤ ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üì∏ 5 Bis by #CharlotteGainsbourg‚ÅÝ
#SergeGainsbourg

posts.caption:
At @luma_arles, @thisispattismith and @soundwalkcollective bring a decade-long collaboration into sharper focus. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Correspondences, their first major European exhibition, moves through field recordings, film, poetry, drawing and archival material to explore ecology, migration, spiritual transmission and the memories held in sound. Across images of endangered landscapes, lost species and human-made disaster, Smith‚Äôs language responds to Stephan Crasneanscki and Simone Merli‚Äôs sonic worlds, while figures including Mary Magdalene and a Siberian shaman offer other modes of communication. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
‚ÄúI was also always happy to be a writer, where I don‚Äôt have to depend on anybody. I just write and it‚Äôs all coming from myself. Now I have both; I have brothers,‚Äù she says of Crasneanscki and sound artist Merli, who joined Soundwalk in 2008. ‚ÄúWe‚Äôre equally loyal collaborators; we‚Äôre of one mind.‚Äù‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Approaching 80, Smith reflects on a creative partnership that continues to open new territory for her writing and for the audience alike. Read @ananda_pellerin‚Äôs interview at the link in bio üì≤‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üì∏ Patti Smith & Stephan Crasneanscki: Correspondences. ¬© Victor&Simon - Gr√©goire d‚ÄôAblon‚ÅÝ

posts.caption:
With a voice as distinctive as ever, @jorjasmith_ returns with her third album, What Are The Odds. ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Set to release in August, the 12-track album, produced by P2J and featuring Wizkid, spans genres ‚Äì from garage to funk, soul and blues. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs about me and my relationships,‚Äù says Smith, cadenced by a Brummie lilt, ‚Äúwhich, to be fair, is what I‚Äôm always writing about.‚Äù From thought to fruition, What Are The Odds has been several years underway, first imagined when working with P2J on her 2023 track Little Things, but actualised in just seven months. She‚Äôs excited to speak about it: ‚ÄúI only ever really want to talk about music.‚Äù ‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Ahead of the album‚Äôs release, @rose_dodd asks Smith 20 quickfire questions on her earliest memories of making music, star signs and life in the public eye. Link in bio üì≤‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
üì∏ Lead image: Photography by Ivor Lawson-Adamah

posts.caption:
PERI ROSENZWEIG ‚Äî THE ARCHITECT OF POP‚ÄôS NEW DARK GLAMOUR
If fashion styling is the art of building identities, Peri Rosenzweig is one of its most compelling world-builders. The New York-based stylist and creative director has quietly become one of the defining visual forces behind contemporary music and fashion, creating the hyper-stylized, futuristic aesthetics shaping a new generation of artists.
From the gothic futurism of Lady Gaga‚Äôs recent era to the glam-industrial universe of Yves Tumor, Rosenzweig‚Äôs work exists at the intersection of couture, underground club culture, punk, fetishwear and science fiction.Her collaborations span some of the most influential names in music and fashion today ‚Äî including Lady Gaga, Yves Tumor, Travis Scott, Julia Fox, Grimes, King Princess, Eartheater, 070 Shake and Charli XCX.
Together with creative partner Nick Royal, she co-founded HARDSTYLE, the image-making studio responsible for some of the most distinctive visual identities in contemporary pop culture, blurring the boundaries between styling, costume design and creative direction.
Rather than dressing celebrities, Rosenzweig creates worlds around them. Her signature aesthetic is unmistakable: dark glamour, post-internet surrealism, subcultural references and a fearless approach to silhouette and texture that feels equally at home in underground nightlife and haute couture.
In an era where image is inseparable from music, Peri Rosenzweig is helping define what the future looks like.
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
üì∑ @perirosenzweig , @terrencefoconnor
#PeriRosenzweig #FashionStylist #CreativeDirection #HARDSTYLE #A2ZFashionMagazine
posts.alt_text:
On 16 July, @rca_fashion‚Äôs Graduation Exhibition 2026 was held at the Kensington campus. Going beyond a traditional fashion school graduation show, the exhibition offered an expo-like experience, showcasing innovative designs that explored the intersection between the physical and digital worlds while reflecting on themes of diversity and identity.

By experimenting with a variety of materials, the exhibition presented works that blurred the boundaries between fashion and art. What I found most fascinating was how each piece transformed depending on the distance ‚Äî from the bold silhouettes seen from afar to the intricate details revealed up close.

In addition, the immersive performances transformed the viewing experience, creating a deeper connection between the works and the audience.

Ultimately, RCA reimagined the traditional graduation showcase into an immersive expo experience, where fashion, art, and emerging technologies came together to explore the future of design. Swipe right to see the highlights.

üìå RCA Fashion Expo 2026
‚Ä¢ Date: 16 (Thurs) - 18 (Sun) July.
‚Ä¢ Time: 12:00 - 18:00.
‚Ä¢ Location: RCA Kensington Campus, SW7 2EU.

Words by Julie Jeong
Edited by Josephine Jiang

Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.

üì∑ @a2z_fashionmag

#RoyalCollegeOfArt #DesignEXPO

posts.caption:
Following the success of their 2025 collaboration, @timberland and @jacquemus reunite to reimagine Timberland‚Äôs iconic 3-Eye Lug Boat Shoe. This time, the collaboration introduces a bold new colour: Lagoon Blue, which was also featured in Jacquemus‚Äô SS27 collection.
The shoe transforms Timberland‚Äôs traditional boat shoe into a contemporary silhouette, blending #Timberland‚Äôs craftsmanship with #Jacquemus‚Äô playful approach through distinctive details such as custom metal charms and aglets.
Through bold colour, quality craftsmanship and Jacquemus‚Äô signature details, this collaboration offers a fresh interpretation of Timberland‚Äôs iconic boat shoe. For those looking to add a fresh and playful touch to their summer style, this is the perfect finishing piece.
Available from 17 July at jacquemus.com, timberland.com, and selected Jacquemus and Timberland¬Æ stores worldwide.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow¬Ý@a2z_fashionmag¬ÝNow.

posts.caption:
On 16 July, @bawwlcf presented its Graduation Show at London College of Fashion, where 43 graduating students showcased collections that expressed their personal identities through critical design, technical skill, and storytelling.
From a collection inspired by the quiet landscapes of a coastal hometown to a contemporary reinterpretation of a 20th-century literary work, each designer transformed personal narratives and perspectives into thoughtful, innovative fashion.
This year‚Äôs show stood out not only for the strength of its concepts, but also for the craftsmanship and attention to detail demonstrated in each collection.
Congratulations to the Class of 2026. A2Z looks forward to seeing you become the next generation of fashion designers and shape the future of fashion.
Words by Julie Jeong
Edited by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
üì∑ @a2z_fashionmag
#UAL #LCF #Womenswear #GraduationShow

posts.caption:
Gap just gave us a reason to fall back in love with denim.
Meet The Hailey Jean ‚Äî a brand-new, limited-edition capsule from Gap, created in collaboration with model and beauty entrepreneur Hailey Bieber. Launching today, July 16th, the collection is a love letter to the effortless, low-maintenance denim style of the late ‚Äò90s ‚Äî the era Hailey says shaped her personal style from the start.
The capsule includes two silhouettes co-designed with Hailey herself: the Extra Baggy Jean, based on a vintage men‚Äôs Gap fit she‚Äôs loved for years, and the ‚Äò90s Low-Rise Loose Jean ‚Äî both available in three washes each. Every pair is made from 100% cotton rigid denim, designed to soften and mold to your body over time, just like a real vintage find. Each pair features custom ‚Äú1996‚Äù hardware and back-patch detailing, a nod to Hailey‚Äôs birth year, along with her signature printed inside the pocket lining.
The campaign itself leans all the way into that ‚Äò90s mood: shot by legendary photographer Mario Sorrenti, styled by Alastair McKimm, and set to The Cranberries‚Äô ‚ÄúLinger‚Äù in an accompanying film directed by Charlie Di Placido.
Priced at $89, ‚ÄúThe Hailey Jean‚Äù drops today at 12pm ET on Gap.com and in select Gap stores across the US, Canada, UK, France, and Japan ‚Äî so if oversized, nostalgic denim is calling your name, you‚Äôll want to move fast on this one. üëñ‚ú®
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.

From the gothic futurism of Lady Gaga‚Äôs recent era to the glam-industrial universe of Yves Tumor, Rosenzweig‚Äôs work exists at the intersection of couture, underground club culture, punk, fetishwear and science fiction.Her collaborations span some of the most influential names in music and fashion today ‚Äî including Lady Gaga, Yves Tumor, Travis Scott, Julia Fox, Grimes, King Princess, Eartheater, 070 Shake and Charli XCX.

Together with creative partner Nick Royal, she co-founded HARDSTYLE, the image-making studio responsible for some of the most distinctive visual identities in contemporary pop culture, blurring the boundaries between styling, costume design and creative direction.

Rather than dressing celebrities, Rosenzweig creates worlds around them. Her signature aesthetic is unmistakable: dark glamour, post-internet surrealism, subcultural references and a fearless approach to silhouette and texture that feels equally at home in underground nightlife and haute couture.

In an era where image is inseparable from music, Peri Rosenzweig is helping define what the future looks like.

Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.

üì∑ @perirosenzweig , @terrencefoconnor

#PeriRosenzweig #FashionStylist #CreativeDirection #HARDSTYLE #A2ZFashionMagazine

posts.caption:
Strength isn‚Äôt always loud. Sometimes, it‚Äôs quiet curiosity, unwavering kindness, and the courage to keep moving forward. Harper‚Äôs Bazaar India‚Äôs June-July cover star, Saffron Vadher (@saffron), reflects on resilience, identity, fashion, and the people who shape us in a conversation that‚Äôs as moving as it is inspiring. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve always believed that life‚Äôs best moments are the ones you share. It‚Äôs never really been about where I am or what I‚Äôm doing. It‚Äôs about who I am doing it with,‚Äù she says.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Harper‚Äôs Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28‚Äîan age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. ‚ÄúIf someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,‚Äù she says. ‚ÄúYet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

Long before it returned to fashion‚Äôs moodboard, Michael Jackson (@michaeljackson) made the Napoleon jacket a cultural icon. Now, the historic silhouette is making a stylish comeback, with celebrities like Margot Robbie and Jenna Ortega (@jennaortega) embracing its sharp tailoring and regal appeal.

posts.caption:
If the 2010s belonged to the ‚Äòcool girl‚Äô, the 2020s belong to the ‚Äòyearner‚Äô. The ‚Äòcool girl‚Äô was never just a personality. She was an expectation. She was told to be low-maintenance, agreeable, and never ask for too much. For years, many women mistook this performance for freedom. Today, they‚Äôre questioning why desirability was tied to shrinking themselves in the first place. Power no longer looks like needing nobody. It looks more like honesty.
Tap the link in bio to read on.
‚úçÔ∏è: Ishani Datta

posts.caption:
After four years of false starts, regulatory hurdles and aborted listing plans in the US and London, Shein is finally on the cusp of going public after receiving regulatory approval from Chinese authorities this week for a Hong Kong listing that could take place as early as next month.
Now the company faces a different problem: convincing investors it deserves a blockbuster valuation.
BoF's Cathaleen Chen (@kittykewlcat) explores whether Shein can convince markets its growth story still holds. Read more #linkinbio
üì∑ Getty Images
posts.alt_text:
This week in fashion:¬ÝRichemont soars, Burberry‚Äôs turnaround gains momentum and Shein‚Äôs IPO ‚Äî plus more industry moves that hit, and missed, the mark.

This Week in Fashion is BoF‚Äôs weekly news briefing, available exclusively to BoF Professional members. Become a BoF Professional member to access the full analysis¬Ý#linkinbio

posts.caption: The lesson in Richemont‚Äôs earnings is value perception. We don‚Äôt talk about ‚Äòvalue‚Äô in luxury, but affluent customers know when they are getting ripped off. Luxury fashion has pushed pricing too far, while jewellery still feels worth the money. There‚Äôs a firm value perception ceiling on a logo T-shirt, much less so on a gold bracelet.

posts.caption:
Earlier this month, Gap rereleased its ‚ÄúHappy Stripe‚Äù design, reimagining the beloved 1990s knit. Many had expected a faithful reinterpretation of the original, only to find that the new garments were composed of 80 percent polyester and 20 percent elastane. The synthetic material make up quickly led to a wave of criticism on social media.
That reaction reflects a broader shift in how consumers are evaluating their clothing. Many are turning away from synthetics, demonising them as little more than cheap plastic, and seeking out products made of natural fibres such as cotton, linen and wool, which are often portrayed as more comfortable and durable.
BoF‚Äôs Shayeza Walid (@shayeza_) explores the consumer shift putting fabric composition under the spotlight. Read more #linkinbio
üì∑ @shopdoen, @pact, @gap, @reformation

posts.caption:
Phia is back in the spotlight, but this time for unwelcome reasons.
Last week, the buzzy fashion-tech startup found itself at the centre of a Bloomberg investigation that revealed it was taking credit for sales it didn‚Äôt actually drive via a tactic called ‚Äúcookie stuffing.‚Äù The story quickly sparked chatter online, in large part because of Phia‚Äôs already high profile, a product of not only its celebrity investors, but also its co-founder Phoebe Gates, daughter of Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates, who launched the business in April 2025 alongside Stanford classmate Sophia Kianni.
Phia told Bloomberg it fixed the issue as soon as they were notified and declined to comment further when contacted by BoF.
BoF's Marc Bain unpacks the bigger picture behind Phia's latest headlines. Read more #linkinbio
‚úçÔ∏è @marcbain_
üì∑ @phiaco

posts.caption:
In 2016, the global retail landscape was dominated by successful direct-to-consumer disruptors like @Everlane, @Glossier, @Allbirds and @OutdoorVoices. Backed by hundreds of millions of dollars in venture capital, they prioritised rapid sales growth and hyper-aggressive social media customer acquisition over immediate profitability.
In this episode, senior news and features editor Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) joins senior correspondent Sheena Butler-Young (@sheena.butler.young) to explore why the once-dominant DTC formula ultimately unravelled ‚Äî and how a quieter, lesser-scrutinised class of brands, such as @ShopD√¥en, @HillHouse and @Staud.Clothing, built more durable businesses by taking a different path.
üéôÔ∏è Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast
üì∑ @hillhouse
posts.alt_text:
After four years of false starts, regulatory hurdles and aborted listing plans in the US and London, Shein is finally on the cusp of going public after receiving regulatory approval from Chinese authorities this week for a Hong Kong listing that could take place as early as next month. 

Now the company faces a different problem: convincing investors it deserves a blockbuster valuation.

BoF's Cathaleen Chen (@kittykewlcat) explores whether Shein can convince markets its growth story still holds. Read more #linkinbio

üì∑ Getty Images

posts.caption:
Romain Spitzer, a 20-year veteran of LVMH‚Äôs fragrance division, will join Kering as CEO of Bottega Veneta, effective September 1.
Spitzer takes the reins of Bottega Veneta following a six-month search for a new leader, after previous chief executive Leo Rongone was named group CEO of Moncler Group in January.
Spitzer got his start as a beauty executive working at Guerlain, Jean-Paul Gaultier and Yves Saint Laurent Parfums. He joined LVMH‚Äôs beauty division in 2006, working on the group‚Äôs flagship Parfums Christain Dior before being named president of LVMH Fragrance Brands, where he oversaw a portfolio of fragrance businesses including Givenchy, Kenzo and Acqua di Parma as well as more recent acquisitions like Maison Francis Kurkdjian and Officine Universelle Buly.
Read more on the appointment #linkinbio
‚úçÔ∏è @r.o.bertwilliams

Some New Yorkers were able to get a closer glimpse at the trophy today at the French maison‚Äôs Fifth Avenue flagship store before it heads to the pitch this weekend.
Created in the storied workshops of Asni√®res-sur-Seine, near Paris, the luxe trunk is covered by the signature Louis Vuitton Monogram canvas, showcasing two front panels adorned with a golden ‚ÄúV‚Äù ‚Äì for ‚ÄúVictory‚Äù and ‚ÄúVuitton‚Äù ‚Äì painted to pay homage to the trophy‚Äôs colors.

Shorts are creeping up. Thigh tattoos are peeking out. Mesh shirts abound. Dare we say it might be straight guy summer? In this weekend's edition of Big Style, Senior Features Reporter T.M. Brown explores how straight men are embracing their sartorial sensuality.

Our weekly Big Style newsletter excavates stylish amusements and contextualizes the world of fashion. Sign up at the link in our bio or comment "Big Style" and we'll link you up.

posts.caption:
How do you define glamour? Like the ever-elusive concept of personal style, glamour is often intangible, innate, even aura-based.
Enter 25-year-old Norwegian soccer player and Manchester City star @erling. Days after being knocked out of the World Cup quarter-finals by England, Haaland and his partner Isabel Haugseng Johansen dusted themselves off and headed to the Mediterranean for the @dolcegabbana Alta Sartoria show in Sicily on Tuesday.
Haaland, who has already caught the world's attention with his sprawling collection of Herm√®s bags and relatable love for a tacky trinket, was dressed to the nines.
He clashed his cream satin pants with a white double-breasted blazer, dark sunshades, a gold frog-shaped brooch and navy woven leather loafers. Most notable was his thin satin-like scarf whipped eccentrically over one shoulder, like a Milanese gentleman oozing sprezzatura.
Not since David Beckham have we seen such pizzazz and flare in soccer fashion.
Read more at the link in our bio.
üì∏: Antonio De Masi/Courtesy Dolce & Gabbana

posts.caption:
Sandra, a 43-year-old media lawyer based in Los Angeles, had been toying with the idea of breast augmentation since her 20s, but was always put off by the pain of the procedure, the long recovery time and the idea of having "this kind of foreign sac in your body."
So when Sandra, who wanted to protect her identity for privacy reasons, heard about a new injectable body contouring product last summer, she was intrigued. No synthetic fillers, no implanted sacs, no downtime.
"You hit a certain age and you decide, I'm going to do something for myself at this point," she said. The product she found ‚Äî alloClae, pronounced allo-clay like the earthy sculpting material ‚Äî is quick to administer. Nicknamed the lunchtime boob-job, it often takes less than an hour to inject and doesn't need general anaesthesia or a hospital setting. Plus, the aftercare instructions, Sandra said, were "shockingly easy." The fact that alloClae uses fat harvested from donated dead bodies, or cadavers as they're referred to in the scientific community, didn't really pose an issue. "I was fine with that," she said.
A growing number of people in the US appear to feel the same. Tiger Aesthetics, the company that manufactures alloClae, said more than 2,000 patients have been injected with it since May 2025. It's a buzzy topic on Reddit's popular plastic surgery page, as potential patients ask questions, seek reviews and swap doctor recommendations.
But for every plastic surgery clinic calling the injectable a "next-generation" advancement, there's an equal number of headlines branding it "zombie filler" or "corpse injections."
If there is a boundary line in the pursuit of the perfect look, are we finally butting up against it? What does it mean if society widely embraces injecting cadaver fat in the service of beauty? Could alloClae become as common as Botox?
Tap the link in bio to read more.
üì∏ Photo illustrations by Connie Chen/CNN/berkpixels/fStop/Getty Images

There are influencers, and then there‚Äôs @madelineargy. Since 2021, the ‚ÄúPretty Lonesome‚Äù host has made eight million strangers feel like her closest friends without even trying. Whether she‚Äôs giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it‚Äôs clear that Argy‚Äôs got range‚Äîand so does her closet.

Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

Tap the link in bio for our latest #ClosetTour to step inside her world, from the breakup that led to her most-treasured boots to why she‚Äôll defend backpacks until the very end.

‚ÄúI want people to feel warm... like they‚Äôre not alone.‚Äù Photographer @rob.tennent has spent his career capturing the tender side of masculinity‚Äîthe glances, the touches, the quiet moments boys aren‚Äôt taught to hold onto. Now based in Paris, he‚Äôs turning his lens on the youth on the edge of adulthood, chasing the same softness that shaped his own boyhood. We caught up with him to chat identity, intimacy, and what he‚Äôs trying to explore through his work.
Photos: @rob.tennent

posts.caption:
Debbie Harry didn‚Äôt just become the face of punk, she also redefined what punk could look like.
At a time when the movement was dominated by aggression and anti-fashion ideals, Harry introduced something unexpected: glamour. She mixed thrift-store finds with designer pieces, leather jackets with sequins, bleach-blonde hair with smeared eyeliner, proving that rebellion and elegance could exist in the same outfit. Her style never felt calculated. It felt instinctive.
As the frontwoman of Blondie, Debbie Harry became one of the first musicians whose wardrobe was as influential as her music. She moved effortlessly between New York‚Äôs underground clubs, the art scene surrounding Andy Warhol, and the emerging world of high fashion, inspiring designers including Stephen Sprouse, Jean Paul Gaultier, Anna Sui and countless others. Decades later, her fingerprints can still be found in collections by houses like Saint Laurent, Gucci and Balenciaga.
What made Harry so influential wasn‚Äôt that she followed trends, it was that she ignored them. She wore vintage before vintage became fashionable, mixed luxury with everyday clothing long before it became standard, and treated personal style as an extension of attitude rather than status.
Long before ‚Äúmodel off-duty‚Äù or ‚Äúdowntown cool‚Äù became fashion clich√©s, Debbie Harry had already perfected them. She didn‚Äôt just influence punk style, she permanently changed the way fashion understands cool.
posts.alt_text:
For a moment, it felt like fashion had travelled back to the era when glamour wasn‚Äôt ironic.

With her Fall 2026 Haute Couture collection, Celia Kritharioti revived the unapologetic supermodel fantasy that defined the late 2000s and early 2010s‚Äîbut refined it through the precision of couture. 
Crystal-encrusted gowns, dramatic feathers, sculptural corsetry and impossibly fluid silhouettes recalled the confidence of the red carpet‚Äôs golden years, when fashion celebrated glamour without apology.

Some of the strongest looks were the liquid silver gowns that clung to the body like molten metal, heavily embellished nude illusions that sparkled from every angle, and architectural white creations that balanced softness with powerful structure. A striking snake-inspired look, intricate lace dresses and feather-trimmed evening gowns added theatricality without overwhelming the collection, while every silhouette maintained the house‚Äôs signature emphasis on femininity and craftsmanship.

What made the collection feel so refreshing wasn‚Äôt nostalgia alone. Rather than simply recreating the glamour of the supermodel era, Kritharioti reminded us why it captivated fashion in the first place: confidence, elegance and the belief that couture should make an unforgettable entrance. In a season dominated by conceptual experimentation, her vision proved that timeless glamour can still feel surprisingly modern. 

Credits

House: Celia Kritharioti
Collection: Fall 2026 Haute Couture
Creative Director: Celia Kritharioti
Photography: Vogue Runway / Filippo Fior, Acielle, Daniele Oberrauch

posts.caption:
When Madonna and Jean-Michel Basquiat met in 1982, neither had reached the extraordinary fame that would later define them.
Their relationship unfolded between late-night clubs, artist lofts, gallery openings and the creative chaos of a city that was reinventing culture in real time. Surrounded by figures like Andy Warhol, Keith Haring and Kenny Scharf, they belonged to a generation that blurred the boundaries between art, fashion and music.
Madonna would later recall waking up in the middle of the night to find Basquiat already painting, completely consumed by his work. His relentless creativity left a lasting impression on her, while Basquiat reportedly admired Madonna‚Äôs ambition and believed she was destined for greatness long before the rest of the world did.
Their romance lasted only a few months, ending as their careers accelerated in different directions. After the breakup, Basquiat publicly asked Madonna to return the paintings he had given her and painted over them in black. Behind his extraordinary talent, however, he was also struggling with severe heroin addiction and the pressures of sudden fame. In 1988, Basquiat died from a heroin overdose at just twenty-seven years old.
Reflecting on his death years later, Madonna admitted she wasn‚Äôt surprised by the news. ‚ÄúHe was so fragile,‚Äù she said, explaining that despite his brilliance, there was always a vulnerability about him that made his tragic fate feel heartbreakingly inevitable.
Looking back, their relationship feels almost symbolic. It captured a brief moment when two future legends crossed paths before history gave them their crowns, a reminder that some of the greatest cultural icons once shared the same uncertain beginning.

posts.caption:
Yves Saint Laurent didn‚Äôt just shape fashion, he also carefully shaped the way it would be remembered.
Away from the runway, the designer spent decades photographing the people, places and moments that surrounded his creative universe. From intimate portraits of friends and muses to quiet snapshots of Marrakech, Paris and his travels, his photographs reveal the same sensitivity that defined his couture. They weren‚Äôt created as fashion campaigns, but as personal memories, fragments of a life lived through beauty, art and observation.
That relationship with photography became just as important as the clothes themselves. Saint Laurent understood that fashion doesn‚Äôt exist in isolation; it lives through the images that preserve it. His own photographs sit alongside iconic portraits made by some of the twentieth century‚Äôs greatest photographers, including Irving Penn, Andy Warhol, Annie Leibovitz, Helmut Newton and Richard Avedon, illustrating how photography became inseparable from his legacy.
More than four decades later, these images feel less like documentation and more like a visual diary. Together they reveal not only one of fashion‚Äôs greatest designers, but the world that inspired him, fascinated him and ultimately became part of fashion history.
Exhibition: Yves Saint Laurent and Photography
Venue: International Center of Photography (ICP), New York
Featuring works by Yves Saint Laurent, Irving Penn, Richard Avedon, Helmut Newton, Annie Leibovitz, Andy Warhol, Sarah Moon and many other photographers from the Yves Saint Laurent archive.

posts.caption:
Some editorials are built around clothes. The best ones are built around an idea.
‚ÄûThe Weight of Others‚Äú explores the invisible pressure of other people‚Äôs judgment and the quiet ways it shapes how we see ourselves. Instead of portraying this feeling literally, it transforms ordinary moments into emotionally charged scenes, revealing how external expectations can turn everyday life into a constant performance.
An overflowing office becomes a metaphor for impossible expectations. A bathroom, usually the most private room in a home, becomes a place of self-observation. Eating dinner or buying an ice cream no longer feels spontaneous but strangely performative, as though every action is being watched. The editorial captures a feeling most people recognize but rarely see visualized: the exhausting awareness of existing through someone else‚Äôs eyes.
Bold flash photography, exaggerated compositions, and vibrant colours blur the line between reality and fiction. Every frame feels slightly surreal, reflecting how anxiety and social pressure distort even the most familiar environments. Rather than documenting everyday life, the images reconstruct it through emotion, making invisible feelings tangible.
What makes The Weight of Others so compelling is its refusal to separate fashion from storytelling. The garments become part of a larger narrative rather than the destination itself, proving that fashion imagery can communicate ideas just as powerfully as it showcases design.
At a time when many editorials prioritize aesthetics alone, The Weight of Others reminds us that the most memorable fashion stories are the ones that leave you with something to think about, not just something to look at.
Brand Name: @gigliotigrrrato
Creative Director of brand and Model: @centodiciotti
Art Director: @fezucchini

Styling: mezzadrijacopo

posts.alt_text:
Debbie Harry didn‚Äôt just become the face of punk, she also redefined what punk could look like.

At a time when the movement was dominated by aggression and anti-fashion ideals, Harry introduced something unexpected: glamour. She mixed thrift-store finds with designer pieces, leather jackets with sequins, bleach-blonde hair with smeared eyeliner, proving that rebellion and elegance could exist in the same outfit. Her style never felt calculated. It felt instinctive.

As the frontwoman of Blondie, Debbie Harry became one of the first musicians whose wardrobe was as influential as her music. She moved effortlessly between New York‚Äôs underground clubs, the art scene surrounding Andy Warhol, and the emerging world of high fashion, inspiring designers including Stephen Sprouse, Jean Paul Gaultier, Anna Sui and countless others. Decades later, her fingerprints can still be found in collections by houses like Saint Laurent, Gucci and Balenciaga.

What made Harry so influential wasn‚Äôt that she followed trends, it was that she ignored them. She wore vintage before vintage became fashionable, mixed luxury with everyday clothing long before it became standard, and treated personal style as an extension of attitude rather than status.

Long before ‚Äúmodel off-duty‚Äù or ‚Äúdowntown cool‚Äù became fashion clich√©s, Debbie Harry had already perfected them. She didn‚Äôt just influence punk style, she permanently changed the way fashion understands cool.

posts.caption:
The most revolutionary piece of couture was never a dress. It was a shoe.
Beginning in the 1920s, Andr√© Perugia transformed footwear from a functional accessory into an artistic discipline. Rejecting the conventional silhouettes of his time, he experimented with sculptural heels, impossible proportions and architectural forms that challenged the very idea of what a shoe could be. His designs weren‚Äôt created to complete an outfit‚Äîthey became the outfit.
Perugia collaborated with some of the greatest couturiers of the twentieth century, including Paul Poiret, Elsa Schiaparelli and Crist√≥bal Balenciaga, while creating shoes for cultural icons such as Josephine Baker and Marlene Dietrich. He approached footwear with the same creative ambition that fashion reserved for haute couture, earning him the reputation as the world‚Äôs first true shoe couturier.
Nearly every luxury shoe designer working today owes something to the path Perugia created. Long before footwear became a statement piece or a collector‚Äôs object, he proved that a shoe could carry as much emotion, craftsmanship and artistic vision as any couture gown.
Modern shoe design didn‚Äôt begin with logos or red soles. It began with Andr√© Perugia.

‚ÅÝ
Led by producer-poet @mikeskinnerltd, the show proved one of the theatrical hip-hop-garage group‚Äôs biggest to date, packing over 14,000 attendees into the outdoor concert area.‚ÅÝ

For generations, blue sapphire has defined the category.
Yet the sapphire family is remarkably diverse, and luxury maisons have long explored that spectrum through exceptional design and storytelling.
As coloured gemstones continue to gain attention in bridal and fashion jewellery, retailers have an opportunity to broaden how they present sapphires‚Äînot by replacing blue, but by expanding the narrative around it.

posts.caption:
Taking a military helicopter to a third grader‚Äôs golf lesson? It‚Äôs giving little prince behavior.
A new MS Now report claims Secret Service agents are ‚Äúfed up‚Äù with JD Vance‚Äôs excessive personal use of government resources and tensions are especially high after he requested a military helicopter fly his nine-year-old son to a golf lesson last week. The trip was ultimately scrapped, not because anyone objected to it, but because of bad weather‚Ä¶and apparently this wasn‚Äôt a one-off.
The Vances have reportedly used military helicopters for multiple personal trips, including house hunting for their growing family, with Usha expecting their fourth child this month. Defense Department budget estimates put helicopter operating costs up to $24,600 per hour and naturally, taxpayers are picking up the tab.
Anonymous Secret Service sources told MS Now the VPs‚Äô requests are not only unprecedented but frequently come at the last minute, throwing agents‚Äô schedules into chaos and forcing rushed security planning. ‚ÄúThey change everything,‚Äù one source said. ‚ÄúThey don‚Äôt stick to their schedules, and that costs shit-tons of taxpayer money.‚Äù
According to the report, agents have become so frustrated with the situation that they‚Äôve even made unofficial merch taking jabs at Vance using his Secret Service code name, ‚ÄúBobcat.‚Äù Coins and stickers with phrases like ‚ÄúBobcat OTR Survivors Club‚Äù and ‚ÄúAdvance. OTR. Repeat.,‚Äù are allegedly circulating as an inside joke.
In a statement to MS Now, the vice president‚Äôs office said, ‚ÄúThe Vances are grateful to the men and women of the U.S. Secret Service who serve our country with distinction.‚Äù
‚ÄúWhile protecting a Vice President with a large policy portfolio and a young and growing family presents a unique challenge, agents of the Secret Service do so with excellence every day.‚Äù
#news #secretservice #threads #reels #dc

posts.caption:
A parasitic outbreak of cyclosporiasis, a gastrointestinal illness, is reportedly affecting thousands of Americans across 31 states, causing diarrhea, nausea, dehydration, and, in some cases, hospitalizations. So far, no deaths have been reported.
Michigan has been hit especially hard, with more than 2,800 reported cases. Health officials say lettuce and other salad greens are a potential culprit, and federal and state investigators are looking into whether Taco Bell is a source of the outbreak.
This comes a year after the Trump administration gutted funding for state and local health departments and scaled back a CDC program that tracks foodborne illnesses üôÉ
#dump #salad #food #news #threads

posts.caption:
Last week, Louis Vuitton won a lawsuit against Molly Tea, a China-based bubble tea brand, for trademark infringement after the tea brand‚Äôs floral logo was found to be too similar to a mark used in Louis Vuitton‚Äôs signature monogram print. The tea company was ordered to pay the French fashion giant the equivalent of $1.5m within 10 days of the ruling. However, the backlash against the ruling has afforded Molly Tea with a huge wave of support, drawing customers to storefronts in a show of solidarity, and sparking fierce criticism of LVMH online,
LVMH‚Äôs grievance with Molly Tea had to do with their use of a logo that looks similar to Louis Vuitton‚Äôs four-petal flower which was developed in the 1890s. While the logo might resemble Louis Vuitton‚Äôs signature pattern, netizens are pointing out the design‚Äôs resemblance to Chinese ancestral artworks and, such as the bao xiang hua motif, and Buddhist symbols which pre-date Louis Vuitton by thousands of years. One RedNote user described the court‚Äôs ruling as ‚Äúcommercial monopolization of a cultural element.‚Äù
Nevertheless, Louis Vuitton has a litigious reputation in China, having filed nearly 60 trademark infringement cases in the country this year, according to the South China Morning Post, including a recent lawsuit against China‚Äôs trademark regulator.
Molly Tea reportedly attempted to acquire trademarks for their four-petal flower logo 17 times since 2022 but faced rejections due to LV‚Äôs trademark ownership of the design. Citing these rejected applications, the Suzhou court handling Louis Vuitton‚Äôs lawsuit said that Molly Tea had ‚Äúsignificant malicious intent‚Äù in continuing to use the logo.
LVMH‚Äôs battle with Molly Tea highlights Europe‚Äôs blind spots as it relates to Asian markets; What a brand may see as a cut-and-dry legal issue, China might see as a larger issue of cultural appropriation. Do historical designs like the four-petal flower belong to tradition or to trademark owners? Should brands even have the power to trademark ubiquitous ancient symbols in the first place?

posts.caption:
Has Haute Couture Fashion Week ever been so copacetic as Fall ‚Äò26? It seems that the designers have reached a point of consensus, so that each show was in harmony with the next.
Of course, The Jonatthieu Anderblazy of it all is hard to ignore. Though the similarities between Dior and Chanel this week became a point of conversation, each was elegant and whimsical, and offered up titillating nods to their house‚Äôs heritages; Chanel‚Äôs ultra blown-up tweed in look 47 and Dior‚Äôs ‚ÄòArizona Coat‚Äô in look 29, for example.
Other little (sometimes very little) details showed up numerous times through the week. Anyone notice the little tiny flower trend? In contrast to the bulging, decadent flowers we are accustomed to seeing on runways, this week‚Äôs florals were wild, pastoral, and lively. In the case of Schiaparelli, who opened the week, they were actually alive. One look used over ten thousand flowers and required almost three thousand hours of work.
Designers also leaned into  molded bodices in a myriad of eye-catching ways. Schiaparelli molded a bodice in glazed silicone which Daniel Roseberry described as an ‚Äúaugmented version of reality.‚Äù The effect was so powerful that the dress flew straight off the runway and onto Zendaya, who was in London for the world premiere of ‚ÄòThe Odyssey.‚Äô Ashi Studio showed a handful of bodices, many of which seemed, frankly, like fodder for 2026 cinema press tours. And Duran Latink, in his debut couture collection for Jean Paul Gaultier, used 3D printing to create a hyperrealistic distorted male torso with hand painted birthmarks and latex nipples.
Congratulations are in order for Pierpaolo Piccioli who showed his first couture collection for Balenciaga. By focusing on the dramatic silhouette of the house and eschewing stunts, he delivered that familiar airy refinement we came to expect from him at Valentino.
In all, this HCFW embraced innovation and history at the same time; Designers practiced using textures like silicon and plastic, they continued to utilize feathers in place of furs, and they pleated their little hearts out to create magnificent displays of craftsmanship. The future of haute couture seems bright.

posts.caption:
Pardon my French, but in couture‚Äôs endless game of who did it first?, let‚Äôs talk about who actually did it first. That would be Ritu Beri.
A prot√©g√©e of embroidery maestro Fran√ßois Lesage, she became the first Indian designer invited as a guest designer on the Haute Couture calendar. She is, and always will be, THAT girl. Very First Lady of the Universe-coded.
On 17 July 1999, she presented her debut couture collection (Fall 1999/2000) at the Indian Embassy in Paris, a venue that would go on to become the backdrop for several of her shows. Behind the runway sat the now-famous Padmapani mural from the Ajanta caves. Twenty-seven years later, the same mural has the girls (grown men) fighting. Foreshadowing? Perhaps. Mrs. Beri has always possessed a curious kind of clairvoyance, the ability to spot the future of Indian fashion and arrive there before anyone else even knew the destination.
The funds were scarce. Even the models had no shoes, only feet dyed in blood-red alta. But this was an era when fashion fairy tales still had a little magic left in them. Hope and audacity could still outrun reality. Unknown designers could still assemble guest lists that included Suzy Menkes, who would go on to rave about Beri in the International Herald Tribune. Those bare red feet became symbols of sensuality. The saris and dresses were hypnotic. The audience was captivated.
Until then, the idea of India in fashion had largely been interpreted through European designers and legacy maisons. This was one of the first moments an Indian designer seized control of the lens through which the Indian story would be told. So now, while everyone wants to be the blueprint, we‚Äôre forgetting who first paved the road from Delhi to Paris. Whose ateliers cracked open the couture calendar and made space for the generations of Indian designers who followed.
In an exclusive t√™te-√Ý-t√™te with DS, Miss ma‚Äôam Beri reflects on the realities of being first, the costs, the courage, and the satisfaction of making history.
Images: Ritu Beri, Spring 2000 Haute Couture.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: Twelve years into her career, success has made Kriti Sanon (@kritisanon) more fearless, but not careless. ‚ÄúI have to instinctively feel that this is something I can‚Äôt let go of,‚Äù she says. The old fear of needing to constantly be on set has faded, replaced by a simple shift: ‚ÄúIt is more about quality versus quantity now.‚Äù
For our Luxury Issue, that idea feels especially relevant. Luxury, at its most meaningful, isn‚Äôt only about access, but discernment: the ability to choose fewer, better things, and to pause without mistaking stillness for disappearance. Sanon doesn‚Äôt speak of this phase as though she‚Äôs arrived. ‚ÄúThis is probably the most exciting phase of my life and my career so far,‚Äù she says, with gratitude in her voice ‚Äî and relief, too.
Click the üîó in bio to read the complete interview.
On @kritisanon: Silk long-sleeve shirt, light wool single-breasted jacket, Blueprint silk twill culottes, Lauren 1980 clutch, all by @newbottega. Razzmatazz earrings, @forestofchintz. Ring by @litmusindia.

posts.alt_text:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStars: Pride is not a June-only conversation. It is shifting beyond visibility and asking what comes next. What does it mean to build communities that outlast individuals? To create institutions instead of exceptions? To leave behind spaces where the next person does not have to start from scratch?

For ELLE‚Äôs Pride portfolio, designer James Ferreira (@jamesferreiralabel), drag king and theatre-maker Siaan (@mxsiaan), athlete Dutee Chand (@duteechand) and transmedia producer Zena Sagar (@zena.sagar)  reflect on the futures they are trying to build. 

Their methods differ, but their ambition is remarkably similar: to leave behind a world more expansive than the one they inherited. Visibility may open the door, but it is community, institutions and shared power that keep it open.

Bringing these stories together is illustrator Vikalp Durga Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra), whose distinct visual language expands the portfolio into a shared queer world of colour, intimacy and imagination.
.
Head to the üîó in bio to read the cover story.
.
Editorial Director: Ainee Nizami Ahmedi (@aineenizamiahmedi) 
Illustration: Vikalp Mishra (@vikalpdurgamishra)
Words by : Tejaswi Subramanian (@tejnesss)
Cover Design: Shrushti Sudhakar (@shrushti_sudhakar)
Editorial Co-ordinator: Sneh Lad (@sneh_lad) 
.
[Pride, Queer Rights]

#ELLECoverStar: Victoria Harbour has been the face of Hong Kong for generations, where the city's soaring skyline meets the water in a view recognised around the world. It sets the stage for our latest cover shoot, with Jacqueline Fernandez (@jacquelienefernandez) bringing her unmistakable presence to one of the city's most iconic locations. As the skyline shifts with the light, she reminds us that the most captivating moments don't always ask for attention. Sometimes, they‚Äôre simply felt. Read our interview with the cover star at the link in bio.
On @jacquelienefernandez: Corset Octopus and Bell pants, both by @_demo_official. Crater ring and Wave amethyst ring, both by @figmenterie. Moo mules by @shopceecee___

posts.caption:
#ELLETeens: A book cover isn‚Äôt just packaging. It‚Äôs part of the story. It‚Äôs why readers collect different editions, obsess over sprayed edges and display books face-out like tiny works of art. So why does every beloved novel suddenly have to become a billboard for its screen adaptation?
From movie tie-in covers to those impossible-to-ignore ‚ÄúNow a Netflix Series‚Äù banners, we‚Äôre talking about why readers can‚Äôt stand seeing books turned into streaming advertisements.
Read more at the üîó in bio.
Words by: @duttachiara13
#Books #BookCovers #MovieAdaptation

#ELLEFashion: Kenyan designer Anil Padia made history this year. The first Kenyan label to reach the finale of the LVMH Prize. But beyond the headline is Yoshita 1967 itself: built on memory, craft, and community, where mirror work, temple bells, and crochet are passed hand to hand through generations of women in Padia‚Äôs Nairobi studio. As Padia says, ‚Äúthe hand cannot be erased‚Äù ‚Äî and neither is the story it tells. Read our full conversation with Padia at the üîó in bio.
Words by: @taronish.batty

posts.caption:
Welcome to the next era of @karolg.
Following her historic Coachella performance in April‚Äîwhere she became the first Latina to headline the annual music festival‚Äîthe Grammy-winning Colombian pop star has been rehearsing endlessly for her biggest international stadium tour yet: Viajando por el Mundo Tropitour. Following her previous Ma√±ana Ser√° Bonito tour‚Äîthe fifth-highest-grossing tour by a Latin artist of all time‚Äîher new show will be both a celebration of the Latin community and an expansion of her sound and performance. ‚ÄúPeople try to pigeonhole me, but I‚Äôm so many things,‚Äù she tells ELLE. ‚ÄúI love that my image and what I represent are deeply Latin, yet I also want to be a global artist.‚Äù
In ELLE‚Äôs Summer 2026 digital cover story, the global superstar opens up about her pride in representing the Latin community, finding herself through love and loss, and teases her next musical era: ‚ÄúThe next version of me will be new to people, but it‚Äôs always been there.‚Äù
Read the full interview at the link in bio.
ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Elliot and Erick Jim√©nez @elliotanderick
Stylist: Nycole Sariol @nycolesariol
Writer: Suzy Exposito @brujacore
Hair: Dindi Hojah at Forward Artists @dindihojah
Makeup: Bo for Dior Beauty @bo_champagne
Production: Nathalie Akiya and Raz Segal at GTS Productions @gtsprod
posts.alt_text:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.

Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.‚Äù) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers‚Äîand stopping to smell them. ‚ÄúIf I‚Äôm not enjoying it, then really, really what‚Äôs wrong with me?‚Äù she tells ELLE. ‚ÄúIf I can‚Äôt have a positive outlook on it when I‚Äôm having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.‚Äù

For ELLE‚Äôs Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she‚Äôs grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: ‚ÄúWe‚Äôre at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.‚Äù Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

ELLE: @elleusa
Editor-in-chief: Nina Garcia @ninagarcia
Photographer: Norman Jean Roy @normanjeanroy
Stylist: Law Roach @luxurylaw
Writer: Kayla Webley Adler @kaylaw
Hair: Orlando Pita at Orlo Salon @hairbyorlandopita @orlosalon
Makeup: Fulvia Farolfi at MA+ Group @fulviafarolfi @maworldgroup
Manicure: Deborah Lippmann for Deborah Lippmann @thelippmannofficial @thedeborahlippmann 
Set design: Marla Weinhoff at 11th House Agency @marlaweinhoff @11thhouseagency

posts.caption:
It‚Äôs been quite a month, but the 2026 #FIFAWorldCup is coming to a stylish close at last. But before attention turns to Sunday‚Äôs final between Spain and Argentina, ELLE editor-in-chief @ninagarcia joined forces with @nbcuniversal News Group chairman @cesarconde and @goldmansachs president John Waldron to celebrate the tournament‚Äôs cultural reach.
On Thursday evening in New York City, a who‚Äôs who of media, fashion, and entertainment gathered to celebrate the World Cup‚Äôs impact far beyond the pitch. Swipe through for a peek inside @telemundo‚Äôs final celebration‚Äîand tap the link in bio for the full look.

Following her previous Ma√±ana Ser√° Bonito tour‚Äîthe fifth-highest-grossing tour by a Latin artist of all time‚Äîher new show will be both a celebration of the Latin community and an expansion of her sound and performance. ‚ÄúPeople try to pigeonhole me, but I‚Äôm so many things,‚Äù she tells ELLE. ‚ÄúI love that my image and what I represent are deeply Latin, yet I also want to be a global artist.‚Äù

In ELLE‚Äôs Summer 2026 digital cover story, the global superstar opens up about her pride in representing the Latin community, finding herself through love and loss, and teases her next musical era: ‚ÄúThe next version of me will be new to people, but it‚Äôs always been there.‚Äù

Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption: Fashion wasn‚Äôt the only thing sparkling during Paris Couture Week. Between the runway shows, the city became a treasure box of extraordinary jewelry unveilings from leading maisons, where inventive settings, vibrant color combinations, and exceptional savoir-faire took center stage. From @mikimoto‚Äôs fire opal brooch mixed with pearls and diamonds to the elevated ‚ÄúSignes and Symboles‚Äù collection from @chanel high jewelry, the standout creations prioritized sculptural silhouettes and extraordinary stone pairings. Here, ELLE executive fashion and accessories director @awolfestyle spotlights the week‚Äôs unforgettable jewelry moments.

posts.caption:
Christopher Nolan has said in interviews that he wanted to emphasise Odysseus‚Äô cunning, inventiveness and capacity for improvisation. The first woman to translate The Odyssey, Emily Wilson complements that vision in the epic poem's opening invocation, inviting readers to see Odysseus not as an untouchable legend, but as fallible, human hero. Wilson‚Äôs edition of The Odyssey‚Äîthe one chosen by Nolan‚Äîstrips away some of the poem‚Äôs apparent marble and restores Odysseus‚Äôs humanity.
Written by: Aditya Mani Jha (@adityamanijha)
#EsquireIndia #TheOdyssey

posts.caption:
Your date might be smiling at you across the table, but she has already clocked the shoes, the body language, the outfit and whether that hug felt natural or like a corporate handshake. The first ten minutes of a date are less about compatibility and more about one simple question: do I want to spend the next two hours with this person or fake an early morning tomorrow? The ick is rarely one dramatic moment. More often it is something you did unknowingly that is insignificant to you can but can suddenly become the reason there isn‚Äôt a second date.
Here is what not to do in those crucial minutes.
Written by: Arshita Suri (@arshitakaursuri)
#EsquireIndia #FirstDate

posts.caption:
‚ÄúHi Shakaila we‚Äôre xyz producers and we‚Äôd love to make a tv show with you‚Äù I‚Äôve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from ü§© to ü´©
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that‚Äôs what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself üòÖ
This black history month especially, I‚Äôm inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it‚Äôs possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That‚Äôs why I‚Äôm so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text:
üíïüíöAfter I went on Lorraine last year I made a silent promise to myself that I would be back at the ITV studios and I had my eyes set on @thismorning. I can‚Äôt believe just a few months later, this promise became a reality.

Of course I was low key stressing üòÖüòÖ but there was absolutely zero reason to be nervous because the entire (and I‚Äôm not exaggerating) team were so warm and lovely that I felt like I was hanging with friends all day. 

Firstly thank you for everyone that tuned in and sent good luck vibesüçÄüíñ! A huge shout out to @lilaflintroberts @sarah_barlow  and @amberstyledit for the opportunity to shine a light on #fashionpsychology and for allowing me to flex my styling skills. 

üíÉüèæüî•I couldn‚Äôt have asked for better models to showcase those amazing looks. @kate_mitchell78 @jazzifizzle53 and @misskspence you KILLT IT 

And last but certainly not least the pros @hollywilloughby and @alisonhammond55 thank you for making me feel so comfortable, it was an honour to watch you both work! 

Stay tuned for a reel on all of the BTS goss but for now I‚Äôm in Estonia about to speak on the biggest stage I have so far. Wish me luck! üçÄüíñ 

Dress: @fumithelabel 
Shoes: @asos 

#Tv #fashionpsychology #press #itv #thismorning

For nearly two decades, our cover star, Yami Gautam Dhar, has built a career on one simple principle: never be predictable. From romantic dramas to thrillers, from characters who quietly endure to women who command the narrative, she has consistently chosen reinvention over repetition. Thoughtful, fearless, and deeply committed to her craft, Yami continues to challenge both herself and her audience with every role she takes on. Just a few reasons why we stan!

‚ÅÝ
Turns out, the brand‚Äôs rerelease pivots garments from cotton to polyester‚Äîand people are calling the brand out, for using the material and for creating clothes in the style of ultra fast fashion brands like SHEIN and Temu üò∂‚Äçüå´Ô∏è‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
These critiques are correct, but there‚Äôs something else that seems to be missing: Gap is a fast fashion brand. While designer collaborations and stunts like this build buzz, they‚Äôre closer to SHEIN than one might think.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Swipe to explore the reaction, how Gap rates, and alternatives to patterned clothing from ‚ÄúGood‚Äù and ‚ÄúGreat‚Äù brands. What are your thoughts on this? Share in the comments, then visit the link for recommendations of brands that embrace patterns ‚Üë‚ÅÝ

posts.alt_text:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands üéâ¬ÝThis is a major accomplishment, as even more brands‚Äîfrom the small and indie through the large and luxury‚Äîare in our Directory.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
What goes into ratings? ü§î A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (‚ÄúWe Avoid‚Äù) to our highest score (‚ÄúGreat‚Äù). Ratings are based solely on a brand‚Äôs transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
How do we select brands to rate? Well‚Ä¶with your help ü•π Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, ‚ÄúGood‚Äù and ‚ÄúGreat‚Äù. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means‚Ä¶6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Here‚Äôs to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers ü•Ç Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ‚ú®‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion‚ÅÝ

posts.caption:
In reviewing newly rated brands over the past three months, we‚Äôve got some good news and bad news üò¨‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
The good news? Of the 431 brands rated, 82 (19%) of rated brands received our two highest scores of ‚ÄúGood‚Äù and ‚ÄúGreat‚Äù. This is an improvement from Q1, where just 8% of the 362 brands scored our highest üí´‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
The bad news? 122 brands rated (28%) received our lowest rating of ‚ÄúWe Avoid‚Äù. This number is technically more than the lowest rated brands in Q1, where 113 (31%) had our lowest rating.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Who are these top performing, more sustainable brands? Swipe to meet them, from bridalwear from the UK to a slow fashion goth company from the US üíö‚ÅÝ

posts.caption:
Wouldn‚Äôt it be nice if you could drop old clothes into your garden and grow a tree? üå≥‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Unfortunately for now, that idea is a fantasy as compostable clothing remains in a distant future due to myriad hurdles. But the demand is there: more and more people are turning to composting as an example of a regenerative future in the present, where composting offers a clever means to keep fashion circular ‚è∫Ô∏è‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Compostable fashion may not be a reality now, but many communities are pushing for this to become a reality‚Äîand brands are taking notice, to mixed results. From Stella McCartney to Under Armor to @wearkent, initiatives are in motion‚Äîbut some are more performative than others.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
If you‚Äôre interested in a regenerative fashion future, watch the composting space. Start by reading a new story by Fru Bekefi at the link, who explores how this technique could emerge in the coming years üå±‚Üë‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
#Fashion #Sustainability #SustainableFashion #Composting #Nature

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus.
A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ‚Äôs @zachbaron, ‚ÄúEvery single location on this movie would‚Äôve been the hardest location on any other movie I‚Äôve ever done‚Ä¶ Every time we‚Äôd go somewhere, we‚Äôd be like, ‚ÄòWell, Iceland will be easier.‚Äô And then it‚Äôs raining sideways and it‚Äôs fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‚ÄòYeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.‚Äô‚Äù
For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: ‚ÄúAll of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren‚Äôt difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.‚Äù
Damon unwraps the ‚Äògift‚Äô that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ‚Äôs summer issue cover story at the üîó in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana
Tailoring by Yelena Travkina and Susanna Badalyan
Key Gaffer by @mick411
Key Grip by @clayton_n_iii
Retouching by @bermudezstudio
Modeling by Kara Smatt
Produced by @campproductions
posts.alt_text:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Robert Pattinson as Antinous. 

Pattinson‚Äôs part in The Odyssey is not big‚Äîhe plays the cockiest of the many suitors who have installed themselves at Ithaca, seeking the hand of Penelope (played by Anne Hathaway) while Matt Damon‚Äôs Odysseus is away. ‚ÄúMy character‚Äôs a little like James Woods in Casino. That was my inspiration for it,‚Äù Pattinson said. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs like a little sleazy. I kept saying at my costume fitting, ‚ÄòI really want to have leopard underpants.‚Äô‚Äù

Pattinson talks to GQ‚Äôs @zachbaron about teaming up with Nolan again for The Odyssey, alongside co-stars Matt Damon and Tom Holland, at the üîó in bio.

posts.caption:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Tom Holland as Telemachus.
When Christopher Nolan first offered Holland a part in The Odyssey, the production dates were identical to those of Spider-Man: Brand New Day (and Holland‚Äôs commitments to the Spider-Man franchise are ironclad). ‚ÄúI said to Chris, like, ‚ÄòLook, I want to do this movie, but if I‚Äôm going to do it, I‚Äôm going to have to call Sony and have a very uncomfortable conversation,‚Äô ‚Äù Holland said. Then he immediately had a very uncomfortable conversation.
‚ÄúChris has that reputation of ‚ÄòThis movie isn‚Äôt going to go five months over, and we aren‚Äôt actually going to lose Tom for two years,‚Äô ‚Äù Holland said. ‚ÄúAny other director, it might have been a slightly different conversation.‚Äù The Odyssey started on the day Nolan said it would and finished nine days early.
For our summer cover story, Holland talks about coming of age on set, getting to star alongside his partner on another project, and how he‚Äôs approaching the future of his career at the üîó in bio.
Story by @zachbaron
Photographs by @alexprager
Styled by @georgecortina
Hair by @teddycharleshair at Nevermind
Skin by @jostrettell at Walter Schupfer Management
Set design by @joneslikeindiana
Tailoring by Yelena Travkina and Susanna Badalyan
Key Gaffer by @mick411
Key Grip by @clayton_n_iii
Retouching by @bermudezstudio
Modeling by Kara Smatt
Produced by @campproductions
posts.alt_text:
Presenting one of our three Summer issue covers with the leading men of @theodysseymovie: Matt Damon as Odysseus. 

A Hollywood veteran of nearly five decades now, Damon has worked on approximately 80 film sets. And yet, as he told GQ‚Äôs @zachbaron, ‚ÄúEvery single location on this movie would‚Äôve been the hardest location on any other movie I‚Äôve ever done‚Ä¶ Every time we‚Äôd go somewhere, we‚Äôd be like, ‚ÄòWell, Iceland will be easier.‚Äô And then it‚Äôs raining sideways and it‚Äôs fucking freezing. Iceland was like, ‚ÄòYeah, easy? Hey, hold my beer.‚Äô‚Äù

For Damon, though, the challenge was part of what made the project so special: ‚ÄúAll of the things that might have been difficult for me at an earlier point in my life weren‚Äôt difficult. They became fun. Like being wet and cold was not a hardship. It was actually something I felt really lucky to be experiencing. It felt temporary and like a gift.‚Äù

Damon unwraps the ‚Äògift‚Äô that was making The Odyssey, alongside his co-stars Robert Pattinson and Tom Holland, and director Christopher Nolan, for GQ‚Äôs summer issue cover story at the üîó in bio.

Story by @zachbaron

posts.caption:
Yesterday, the Government announced Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) will be added to the newborn blood spot test for all babies in England from October, after fierce campaigning by singer Jesy Nelson. Her twins were diagnosed with SMA Type 1 six months after she gave birth, for a condition where medical intervention at the earliest possible moment is vital.
‚ÄòIt‚Äôs a bittersweet feeling,‚Äô she tells Grazia in a new interview. Now, she‚Äôs campaigning for testing to be rolled out across Wales and Northern Ireland too. ‚ÄòEvery baby matters,‚Äô she says. ‚ÄòWe‚Äôre talking about whether they‚Äôre going to die or not.'
TAP the link in the bio to read more.

posts.caption: For the last century, the fashion world has been interpreting and reinterpreting ancient Greek attire, to the point where it‚Äôs almost impossible to outline the ways Classical references have influenced the way we dress. In fact, Greek fashion is build directly into the ethos of modern wardrobes. The ‚Äúchiton‚Äù and ‚Äúpeplos,‚Äù the two archetypal styles of tunics worn in ancient Greece‚Äîthe ones you think of around Halloween‚Äîset a precedent for clothing that freed the human form from constriction regardless of gender‚Äîlong before @luxurylaw and @zendaya masterfully interpreted these codes for #TheOdyssey press tour. From gladiator sandals to goddess gowns, at the link in our bio Bazaar's @Camille_freestone unpacks all the ways Grecian influence shows up in modern style. How do you see the ancient civilization's influence play out in your everyday style?

‚ÅÝ

posts.alt_text:
For @mountainhardwear, performance and style go hand in hand when it comes to climbing 

What once existed primarily on cliff faces and bouldering walls, as a small, tight-knit community, now influences everything from design and fashion to how people think about movement and adventure. 

Spending decades building alongside athletes, the outdoor brand is bringing that classic climbing feel to the contemporary conversation. With its new ‚ÄúFloat On‚Äù collection, the staple outdoor brand balances everyday style with technical performance, designing pieces that are made for life on and off the wall. 

Tested through repetition, failure, and success, Mountain Hardwear‚Äôs latest line is built to carry you from the base to your personal summit.

Click the link in bio for more.

üé§: @easternspy 
üé•: @y.lanyi + @dillfiles 
Special thanks to: @metrorock_bushwick

posts.caption:
Sliced bread. The wheel. Reese's Peanut Butter Cups. @chanelofficial Pre-Fall/Winter 2026 carpenter jeans. Some inventions simply can't be improved upon, and Matthieu Blazy's clever twist on a workwear classic might just belong in that company. At first glance, they're everything you'd expect from a pair of carpenter jeans: relaxed, practical, and pocket-heavy. Look closer, though, and the magic reveals itself. Just off the left back pocket, Chanel's interlocking CC logo doubles as an impossibly elegant hammer loop, a small detail that quietly steals the show.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
That subtle flourish sums up why Blazy's vision for Chanel feels so compelling. Rather than relying on spectacle, he elevates familiar silhouettes through thoughtful craftsmanship. Carpenter jeans have long evolved from job-site essentials into fashion staples, but Chanel's version refines the formula with a detail so deceptively simple it makes even a $2,800 price tag feel part of the fantasy.‚ÅÝ

posts.caption:
We made a trip to Tuscany to check out the legacy and process behind @miumiu‚Äòs Matelass√© bags firsthand. From the archive that houses its history to the workshop floor where a single leather panel becomes a Wander or perhaps an Arcadie, here's everything we saw along the way.
Credit: Miu Miu / Hypebae
On a Fall/Winter 2006 Paris Fashion Week runway, Miu Miu introduced a leather so distinct in texture and attitude that it would go on to define the house's design vocabulary, the Matelass√© technique.
Our visit began with a tour of the Prada Group's industrial headquarters designed by architect Guido Canali. Built primarily from steel, the complex holds a near-equal balance of structure and greenery.
The Matelass√© origin story came out of an experiment, and once it hit the runway, it took off in a way no one on the design team fully anticipated at the time.
From the main entrance, we moved into the archive; a room where decades of Miu Miu history live side by side.
The archive holds an estimated 22,100 Miu Miu pieces, including 6,500 handbags.
The design team visits the archive daily for reference, and pieces regularly leave the archive for exhibitions, campaigns and events in Milan and beyond.
The Matelass√© origin story came out of an experiment, and once it hit the runway, it took off in a way no one on the design team fully anticipated at the time.
The technique begins with a machine program that stitches across the leather panel, and once it's cut away, an elastic lycra layer underneath pulls the surface inward into the raised, textured pattern the process takes its name from.
Every pattern, every panel, every piece of a Matelass√© bag is worked out in 2D design programs before it ever becomes a physical object.
Once cut, all the components for a single bag are boxed together and handed off to an assembly operator. A single Wander bag is built from roughly 100 individual pieces.

Jake Shane is logging off, locking in his angles, and entering his wellness era. ‚ÄºÔ∏è
We sat down with the internet‚Äôs favorite unofficial therapist, @passthatpuss, to talk about everything from what a true friend means to him to what actually goes into the science of a flawless thirst trap.
Wellness, to Shane, means finding moments away from the noise to unplug entirely. posts.caption:
You may have noticed an influx in custom World Cup outfits on your timeline this past month, and there‚Äôs a reason. Since @tolami_benson lit up Euro 2024 in her bespoke looks from @antonia.bronze and @hattiecrowther, WAG fashion has been defined by the art of upcycling.
Some of the best looks of the tournament so far have come from our favorite WAGs, working with different designers to transform jerseys into one-of-a-kind statement pieces for each game.  From Norway to Brazil, WAGs are officially fashion icons again, and they‚Äôre changing the way fans dress up, too.
Hypebae‚Äôs Associate Editor @s.imi gives us a brief timeline of WAG style and where the new obsession with upcycling fits in. Swipe to see some of the best looks of the tournament.
Imagery: Courtesy of Brands

posts.caption:
@callumscars_ heads to Goodwood Festival of Speed as the most ambitious @lego Technic Ultimate Car concept hits the hill climb.
Captured in a new episode of 'Come with Me,' the car journalist discovers the latest LEGO x @koenigsegg collaboration which recreates its powerful Sadair's Spear megacar in both a life-sized car and 1:8 model.
After putting the 327,000-piece big build into ghost mode at the paddock, he watches the car take on the infamous Goodwood Hill, where it topped records as the fastest LEGO build, hitting 111km/h with Markus Lundh at the wheel.
To find out more, head to Hypebeast.com
In partnership with LEGO

posts.caption:
@swatch is celebrating the historic Apollo 11 moon landing by dropping its most exclusive collab with @omega: the MoonSwatch Mission to the Moon 1969 üåï‚ú®‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
Limited to just 1,969 individually numbered pieces, Swatch melted down period-correct vintage OMEGA spare parts from the era to create 11 grams of 18K Moonshine Gold for each dial, crown, and pusher.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
The watch features a matte black Bioceramic case, a gold-finished tachymeter bezel, and a custom footprint battery cover.‚ÅÝ
‚ÅÝ
To cop one, you have to fill out an online ESTA application by July 21 for a chance to buy it at $570 USD, a price based on the actual cost of gold in 1969.‚ÅÝ
Photo: Swatch/OMEGA

@ahsaassy_ steps into the season‚Äôs softest chapter, where easy silhouettes and unhurried afternoons become a reminder that slowing down is its own kind of luxury.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this explores the evolving moods of summer, from nature‚Äôs quiet embrace to the intensity of the afternoon sun, and the effortless cool of golden evenings.‚ú®
This is a celebration of the season in all its shades, beginning with the calm before the heat, where style isn‚Äôt about standing out, but feeling completely at home in your own skin.

posts.caption:
@jennifer.garner *always* knows what to do (and it usually involves sorghum).
Between starring in summer‚Äôs buzziest new series, taking her near-billion dollar company public, and turning ‚ÄòPretend Cooking Show‚Äô into a reality, she still manages to be the nicest woman in Hollywood.
Playing a widowed food blogger in @Peacock‚Äôs adaptation of @elinhilderbrand‚Äôs bestseller ‚ÄòThe Five-Star Weekend,‚Äô the beloved actress and entrepreneur channels her inner Ina Garten for a not-so-Instagrammable girls‚Äô weekend on Nantucket with co-stars-turned-IRL besties, @chloesevigny, @morereginahall, @gemmachan and @darcycarden, exploring what it means to grow and develop in middle age.
At 54, the Marvel Hero and supermom reflects on her genre-spanning career, being ‚Äútotal besties‚Äù with John Cena, her surprisingly vast knowledge of weaponry, plus the responsibility of celebrities to stand up for working locally for InStyle‚Äôs Summer Imagemakers Issue at the link in @instylemagazine‚Äôs bio now.
